title: Mock Test 2 - Pressure Test
description: Thi thử áp lực: Câu hỏi khó hơn, toàn diện hơn. Rèn luyện tư duy nhanh dưới áp lực thời gian.

questions.prompt: A Markov chain has transition matrix [[0.7,0.3],[0.4,0.6]]. What is the stationary distribution?
The gradient of f(x,y) = x^2*y + y^3 at point (1,2) is:
Two variables X,Y have covariance 6, variance of X = 4, variance of Y = 9. What is the Pearson correlation?
What is the softmax of [1, 2, 3] (to 2 decimal places)?
A test for disease has sensitivity=0.95, specificity=0.90. Disease prevalence=1%. You test positive. What is P(disease | positive)?
What is the KL divergence KL(P||Q) when P=Q?
questions.explanation: KL divergence = 0 when P and Q are identical distributions. It measures how P differs from Q.

questions.prompt: A matrix A is positive semi-definite (PSD) if and only if:
questions.explanation: A matrix is PSD iff all eigenvalues >= 0. PSD matrices arise in covariance matrices, kernel methods.

questions.prompt: What is the entropy H(X) of a fair 4-sided die?
questions.explanation: For uniform distribution over n outcomes: H = log2(n). For n=4: H = log2(4) = 2 bits.

questions.prompt: Cross-entropy loss for true label y=1, predicted probability p=0.8 is:
questions.explanation: Binary cross-entropy: L = -y*log(p) - (1-y)*log(1-p). For y=1: L = -log(0.8) = -ln(0.8) ≈ 0.223.

questions.prompt: Which optimization algorithm adapts learning rates per parameter using estimates of first and second moments?
questions.explanation: Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) maintains per-parameter adaptive learning rates using exponential moving averages of gradients and squared gradients.

questions.prompt: What is the variance of the dataset [2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9]?
questions.explanation: Mean=5.0. Squared deviations: 9,1,1,1,0,0,4,16. Sum=32. Variance=32/8=4.0.

questions.prompt: A linear regression model y = 2x + 1 predicts on x=3. Actual y=8. What is the residual?
questions.explanation: Predicted = 2*3+1 = 7. Residual = actual - predicted = 8 - 7 = 1.

questions.prompt: Which matrix decomposition is used in PCA to find principal components?
questions.explanation: PCA uses eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix (or equivalently SVD of the data matrix) to find principal components.

questions.prompt: A 95% confidence interval means:

options.text: There is a 95% probability the true parameter lies in this interval
If we repeat the procedure many times, 95% of constructed intervals will contain the true parameter
The sample mean is within 95% of the true mean
95% of data points fall in this interval

questions.explanation: Frequentist CI: if we repeated sampling many times, 95% of computed intervals would contain the true parameter.

questions.prompt: What is the number of parameters in an LSTM unit with input size d and hidden size h?
questions.explanation: LSTM has 4 gates. Each gate has weight matrices W_x (d x h), W_h (h x h), and bias (h). Total = 4*(d*h + h*h + h).

questions.prompt:
What is the output?

def gen():
    yield 1
    yield 2
    yield 3
g = gen()
next(g)
print(list(g))
questions.explanation: next(g) consumes the first value (1). list(g) drains remaining values: [2, 3].

questions.prompt:
What does this code return?

import numpy as np
a = np.array([1,2,3,4,5,6]).reshape(2,3)
print(a[:,1])
questions.explanation: a is [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]. a[:,1] selects column index 1: [2,5].

questions.prompt:
What will the following code print?

class A:
    x = 10
a1 = A()
a2 = A()
a1.x = 20
print(A.x, a1.x, a2.x)
questions.explanation: a1.x=20 creates an instance attribute on a1 only. Class attribute A.x stays 10. a2.x still references class attr: 10.

questions.prompt: Which pandas operation efficiently computes a rolling 7-day average?
questions.explanation: rolling(7).mean() computes a sliding window of 7 periods average, perfect for rolling averages.

questions.prompt:
What is the output of:

from functools import reduce
result = reduce(lambda x, y: x * y, [1,2,3,4,5])
print(result)
questions.explanation: reduce applies lambda cumulatively: ((((1*2)*3)*4)*5) = 120.

questions.prompt: Which numpy function stacks arrays vertically (along axis 0)?
questions.explanation: np.vstack stacks arrays vertically (row-wise). Equivalent to np.concatenate with axis=0 for 2D arrays.

questions.prompt:
What does the following sklearn Pipeline do?

Pipeline([("scaler", StandardScaler()), ("clf", LogisticRegression())])

options.text: Applies StandardScaler then LogisticRegression in sequence, preventing data leakage
Only the last step is applied

questions.explanation: sklearn Pipeline chains steps in order: StandardScaler transforms, then LogisticRegression trains. Crucially, scaler is fit only on training data.

questions.prompt: What is the result of np.dot(np.array([[1,2],[3,4]]), np.array([[5,6],[7,8]]))?
What does @property decorator do in Python?

options.text: Makes a method a class method
Allows a method to be accessed like an attribute without calling it with ()

questions.explanation: @property turns a method into a managed attribute. obj.value calls the getter without parentheses.

questions.prompt: Which pandas function tests whether two DataFrames are element-wise equal (handling NaN)?
questions.explanation: df.equals() compares element-wise and treats two NaN values as equal. == returns a boolean DataFrame.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of sorting a list of n elements using Python's built-in sort?
questions.explanation: Python uses Timsort, which runs in O(n log n) worst case and O(n) best case (already sorted).

questions.prompt:
What does the following code return?

data = [{"name":"A","score":90},{"name":"B","score":75},{"name":"C","score":85}]
sorted(data, key=lambda x: x["score"], reverse=True)[0]["name"]
questions.explanation: Sorted by score descending: A(90),C(85),B(75). First element is A. ["name"] = "A".

questions.prompt: In numpy, what does arr[arr > 5] return?

options.text: Elements of arr that are greater than 5

questions.explanation: Boolean indexing with arr[condition] returns the actual elements satisfying the condition.

questions.prompt: Which Python data structure provides O(1) access to both ends (append and pop from left and right)?
questions.explanation: collections.deque provides O(1) append/pop from both ends. Regular list has O(n) pop/insert at position 0.

questions.prompt: What does pd.get_dummies(df, columns=["color"]) do?

options.text: One-hot encodes the "color" column into binary indicator columns

questions.explanation: get_dummies performs one-hot encoding: creates a binary column for each unique value in "color".

questions.prompt: What does np.einsum("ij,jk->ik", A, B) compute?
questions.explanation: einsum "ij,jk->ik" sums over j (the shared dimension), which is exactly matrix multiplication A @ B.

questions.prompt: Which method on a pandas DataFrame returns the number of non-null values per column?
questions.explanation: df.count() returns the number of non-null values for each column. df.notnull().sum() is equivalent but more explicit.

questions.prompt: What is a Python closure?

options.text: A function defined inside a class
A function that captures and remembers variables from its enclosing scope
A finally block in exception handling

questions.explanation: A closure is an inner function that references variables from an outer function's scope, even after the outer function returns.
nonlocal x refers to f's x=10. g increments to 11 and returns 11. Global x remains 5. Output: "11 5".

questions.prompt: Which scipy function performs a two-sample t-test for independent samples?
questions.explanation: scipy.stats.ttest_ind performs the independent samples t-test comparing means of two groups.

questions.prompt: What is the computational complexity of computing attention in Transformers with sequence length n?
questions.explanation: Standard self-attention computes pairwise dot products between all positions: O(n^2) in memory and time.

questions.prompt: In gradient boosting, what does each new tree model?

options.text: The residual errors (negative gradients) of the previous ensemble
The mean of all previous predictions

questions.explanation: Gradient boosting fits each new tree to the pseudo-residuals (negative gradient of loss) of the current ensemble.

questions.prompt: What is "mixture of experts" (MoE) in LLMs?

options.text: A model architecture where different parameter subsets (experts) are activated for different inputs

questions.explanation: MoE routes tokens to different expert sub-networks via a gating mechanism. Only a subset of parameters activates per token.

questions.prompt: Which technique in RAG improves retrieval by generating multiple search queries from one user query?
questions.explanation: Multi-query retrieval generates several query variations and combines retrieved results, improving recall.

questions.prompt: In contrastive learning (e.g., SimCLR), the model learns by:

options.text: Pulling together representations of augmented views of the same image while pushing apart different images
Maximizing the distance between all pairs
Minimizing reconstruction error like an autoencoder

questions.explanation: Contrastive learning uses positive pairs (same image, different augmentation) vs. negative pairs to learn representations.

questions.prompt: What does "speculative decoding" improve in LLM inference?

options.text: Inference throughput by using a small draft model to propose tokens verified by the large model

questions.explanation: Speculative decoding uses a fast small model to generate token candidates; the large model verifies in parallel, increasing throughput.

questions.prompt: In MLOps, what is "model staleness"?

options.text: A model that is too large to deploy
Performance degradation due to a model not being updated while real-world data distributions shift
A model trained on outdated hardware

questions.explanation: Model staleness occurs when the world changes but the model is not retrained, causing drift in predictions.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes semantic search from keyword search?

options.text: Semantic search understands meaning/intent; keyword search matches exact tokens
Semantic search only works in English

questions.explanation: Semantic search uses embeddings to find conceptually similar content even with different wording. Keyword search requires exact matches.

questions.prompt: Which PEFT (Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning) method inserts low-rank matrices into transformer layers?
questions.explanation: LoRA decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices (A*B), dramatically reducing trainable parameters.

questions.prompt: In information theory, what is the mutual information I(X;Y)?

options.text: The entropy of X plus entropy of Y
The reduction in uncertainty about X given knowledge of Y
The correlation between X and Y

questions.explanation: Mutual information I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y): how much knowing Y reduces uncertainty about X.

questions.prompt: What is "chain-of-thought prompting" in LLMs?

options.text: Encouraging the model to reason step-by-step before giving a final answer
Using a sequence of prompts to fine-tune a model
Connecting multiple prompts with logical operators

questions.explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al. 2022) asks LLMs to show intermediate reasoning steps, improving complex task performance.

questions.prompt: What does "population stability index" (PSI) measure in model monitoring?

options.text: Distribution shift between training data and current production data

questions.explanation: PSI quantifies how much the distribution of a feature or prediction score has shifted. PSI > 0.2 signals significant drift.

questions.prompt: In active learning, which samples are most valuable to label?

options.text: The samples the model is most uncertain about
The most common samples in the dataset
Samples closest to the training mean

questions.explanation: Active learning queries the oracle for labels on the most uncertain samples, maximizing information gain per label.

questions.prompt: What is "catastrophic forgetting" mitigation technique "elastic weight consolidation" (EWC)?

options.text: Penalizing changes to weights important for previous tasks based on their Fisher information
Saving and restoring previous model checkpoints

questions.explanation: EWC adds a regularization term that penalizes modification of weights that were important for previous tasks.

questions.prompt: Which technique reduces the memory footprint of LLMs by representing weights in lower precision (e.g., 4-bit)?
questions.explanation: Quantization converts full-precision (FP32/FP16) weights to lower precision (INT8/INT4), reducing memory and speeding inference.

questions.prompt: What is the "lost in the middle" problem in LLMs?

options.text: LLMs lose track of conversation history after many turns
LLMs tend to underweight information in the middle of long context windows relative to beginning/end
LLM responses truncated at maximum token length
Training loss plateaus in the middle of training

questions.explanation: Research shows LLMs perform worse when relevant information is in the middle of a long context vs. at beginning or end.

questions.prompt: In time-series forecasting, which model captures long-term dependencies using gating mechanisms?
questions.explanation: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) uses input, forget, and output gates to selectively remember long-range dependencies.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of "negative sampling" in training word2vec?

options.text: Efficiently approximate the full softmax by sampling k negative examples instead of all vocabulary

questions.explanation: Negative sampling trains word2vec by distinguishing true context words from randomly sampled "negative" words, avoiding full softmax computation.

questions.prompt: An AI system is trained on historical police data that reflects biased policing practices. The resulting predictions will likely:

options.text: Be unbiased since they are data-driven
Amplify and perpetuate the historical bias
Only be biased if the developer intended bias

questions.explanation: ML models learn patterns from data. If training data contains historical bias, models will perpetuate or amplify those biases.

questions.prompt: Which is an example of "Goodhart's Law" in AI?

options.text: A model improves on a benchmark by genuinely solving the underlying problem
A model achieves high click-through rate by showing sensationalist content rather than quality content
Training loss decreasing while test loss increases

questions.explanation: Goodhart's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. The model optimizes the metric, not the intent.

questions.prompt: What is the logical error in: "Studies show coffee drinkers earn more. Therefore, drinking coffee causes higher earnings."?

options.text: Correlation vs. causation / confounding variable error

questions.explanation: Correlation does not imply causation. A confounder (e.g., professional environments) may explain both coffee drinking and earnings.

questions.prompt: Under EU AI Act, which category requires the highest level of scrutiny?

options.text: High risk AI (e.g., CV screening, medical diagnosis)

questions.explanation: The EU AI Act prohibits unacceptable risk AI outright (e.g., social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance in public). High risk requires compliance but is allowed.

questions.prompt: What does "sycophancy" in LLMs refer to?

options.text: Models that repeat user input verbatim
Models that agree with or flatter users even when incorrect, prioritizing approval over accuracy
Models that generate overly long responses
Models trained on social media data

questions.explanation: Sycophantic LLMs tell users what they want to hear. RLHF can accidentally reinforce this if human raters prefer agreeable responses.

questions.prompt: Which logical form correctly represents "If and only if" (biconditional)?
questions.explanation: Biconditional P <-> Q means P->Q AND Q->P. True only when both P and Q have the same truth value.

questions.prompt: What is "responsible scaling policy" (RSP) in AI safety?

options.text: A policy requiring smaller models to scale gradually
A commitment to pause or limit capability scaling if certain safety thresholds are exceeded
An EU regulation on AI compute usage
A method for reducing carbon footprint of training

questions.explanation: RSPs (e.g., Anthropic's ASL policy) define safety thresholds; if crossed during capability scaling, deployment is paused pending safety measures.
